subject: Cardiology
title: Acyanotic congenital heart defects
preambleMarkdown:
# Acyanotic congenital heart defects

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Acyanotic congenital heart defects covers congenital structural heart lesions. Exam questions emphasize fetal or postnatal physiology, shunt direction, cyanosis, murmur pattern, prostaglandin-responsive ductal lesions, pulmonary overcirculation, Eisenmenger physiology, and timing of specialist referral.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Cyanosis, shock, and severe respiratory distress in a neonate require urgent evaluation.
- Pulse oximetry screening detects many critical lesions but not all.
- Prostaglandin E1 can maintain ductal patency in selected critical lesions.
- Echocardiography is the key diagnostic test.
- Adults with repaired congenital heart disease still need lifelong follow-up.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Acyanotic congenital heart defects refers to congenital cardiac malformations grouped by whether systemic arterial oxygen saturation is usually preserved or reduced.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Left-to-right shunts.
- Obstructive lesions.
- Cyanotic mixing lesions.
- Ductal-dependent pulmonary or systemic blood flow.
- Repaired versus unrepaired adult congenital heart disease.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Congenital heart disease ranges from neonatal critical lesions to repaired adult congenital disease. Exams emphasize newborn cyanosis or shock, pulse-ox screening misses, syndromic associations, and adult complications after childhood repair.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Anatomy centers on septa, valves, outflow tracts, great-vessel connections, ductus arteriosus, pulmonary blood flow, systemic blood flow, and surgical repairs. Small anatomic differences determine whether the lesion is cyanotic, ductal-dependent, obstructive, or a left-to-right shunt.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Clinical findings follow shunt direction, pulmonary vascular resistance, systemic vascular resistance, ductal patency, and ventricular loading. Falling pulmonary vascular resistance after birth can unmask pulmonary overcirculation, while ductal closure can precipitate shock in ductal-dependent lesions.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Lesions cause mixing, obstruction, left-to-right shunting, right-to-left shunting, pulmonary overcirculation, cyanosis, heart failure, arrhythmia, or pulmonary vascular remodeling. Long-standing unrepaired shunts can progress to Eisenmenger physiology.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Many cases are multifactorial. Associations include chromosomal syndromes, maternal diabetes, teratogen exposure, congenital rubella, familial disease, and developmental errors in septation, conotruncal rotation, valve formation, or great-vessel alignment.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess family history, maternal diabetes, teratogens, congenital infection, abnormal fetal ultrasound, syndromic features, cyanosis, feeding difficulty, failure to thrive, and prior congenital repair.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Murmur, cyanosis, tachypnea, poor feeding, failure to thrive, differential pulses, clubbing, exercise intolerance, arrhythmia, or heart failure.
- Some lesions are asymptomatic until adulthood.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Neonatal cyanosis not corrected by oxygen.
- Shock after ductal closure.
- Absent or weak femoral pulses.
- Single loud S2 with cyanosis.
- Syncope or arrhythmia in repaired congenital disease.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Cyanosis and neonatal distress can reflect lung disease, sepsis, persistent pulmonary hypertension, methemoglobinemia, airway disease, or shock. Murmurs can be innocent, but poor perfusion, cyanosis, or weak femoral pulses are not benign.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use pulse oximetry, four-extremity blood pressures when relevant, ECG, chest radiograph, and echocardiography.
- Use cardiac MRI, CT, catheterization, or genetic testing selectively.
- Assess for syndromic features and extracardiac anomalies.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use pulse oximetry, four-extremity blood pressures when relevant, ECG, chest radiograph, and echocardiography. CT, MRI, catheterization, genetic testing, and oxygen/hyperoxia testing are used selectively with specialist input.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, temperature, and perfusion.
- Use prostaglandin E1 when ductal-dependent disease is suspected.
- Treat heart failure and pulmonary overcirculation while arranging pediatric or adult congenital cardiology care.
- Plan catheter or surgical correction based on lesion and timing.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Tetralogy of Fallot is the classic cyanotic lesion.
- VSD is a common acyanotic lesion.
- Coarctation causes upper-lower extremity pulse or blood-pressure differences.
- Eisenmenger syndrome reverses a left-to-right shunt after pulmonary vascular disease develops.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: step-up-emergency-medicine import start -->
Source: Step-Up to Emergency Medicine. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Congenital Heart Disease

_Source lines: 28681-28946._

```text
VII. CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE
A. Discussion. Many types of congenital heart disease are appreciated and are be-
yond the scope of this text. Only those entities that are likely to present to an ED
are covered here.

1. Incidence. Congenital heart disease occurs in approximately 8 in 1,000 live
births; 2 to 4 of those 1,000 newborn infants are symptomatic with heart dis-
ease in the first year of life.

a. The diagnosis is established by 1 week of age in 40% to 50% of patients
with congenital heart disease.

b. The diagnosis is made by 1 month of age in 60% to 70% of infants with
serious congenital heart disease.

2. Types. Congenital heart disease can be divided into cyanotic and acyanotic
types.

a. Acyanotic. The majority of cases of congenital heart disease involve acya-
notic lesions that are usually discovered on examination for other reasons.

They usually are not the reason for an ED visit.

b. Cyanotic. The cyanotic congenital heart disease variants are true life-
threatening emergencies and often result in such children presenting to
the ED in extremis. Cyanosis results when obstruction to right ventricular
outflow causes shunting at the cardiac level from right to left or when left
ventricular outflow is impeded. Because of decreased ventilatory reserve,
profound acidosis is common. Circulatory collapse is imminent after the
development of acidosis.

354 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pediatric Emergencies
B. Clinical features
1. Cyanotic lesions associated with decreased pulmonary blood flow
a. Tetralogy of Fallot is the most common cyanotic congenital heart defect,
accounting for 10% of congenital defects.

i. Pathogenesis. The primary lesion involves hypoplasia of the infundibu-
lum, resulting in outflow tract obstruction. Variable right ventricular
outflow obstruction results in functional pulmonary stenosis. Dextropo-
sition of the aorta results in overriding of the ventricular septum. Ventric-
ular septal defect and right ventricular hypertrophy may also be evident.

ii. Manifestations reflect the degree of severity of the right ventricular out-
flow obstruction.

a) Cyanosis is variable and may be absent.

b) Hypoxemic or "tet spells" characterized by irritability, hyperpnea, cy-
anosis, and syncope occur and may be fatal if not managed properly.

c) A harsh systolic ejection murmur is often heard along the sternal border.

d) Because of the functional pulmonary banding that occurs with the
anatomy of this syndrome, CHF does not usually develop.

b. Pulmonary atresia with or without ventricular septal defect is an ex-
tremely severe form of tetralogy of Fallot.

i. Pathogenesis. The main pulmonary artery is atretic, and pulmonary
blood flow depends entirely on a patent ductus arteriosus or bronchial
collateral vessels.

ii. Manifestations. Patients present with findings of severe cyanosis and
are usually profoundly ill. Often no murmur is heard, and a single first
heart sound (S1 ) is all that is appreciated in the syndrome when associ-
ated with a ventricular septal defect.

c. Transposition of the great vessels with ventricular septal defect and pul-
monary stenosis may mimic tetralogy of Fallot in clinical presentation.

i. Pathogenesis. This condition is caused by transposition of the vessels;
therefore, the obstruction is in the left ventricle instead of the right ven-
tricle. The heart is usually enlarged, and there may be pulmonary con-
gestion, depending on the degree of pulmonary stenosis.

ii. Manifestations. The most common presentation is poor feeding and
lethargy, with mild cyanosis present on physical examination.

d. Ebstein anomaly of the tricuspid valve
i. Pathogenesis. Ebstein anomaly is caused by downward displacement of
the right atrium and tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, which re-
sults in an enormous right atrium and a regurgitant tricuspid valve.

ii. Manifestations. The severity of symptoms depends on the degree of dis-
placement of the tricuspid valve.

a) In most patients, the only complaint is fatigue and cardiac dysrhyth-
mias, of which paroxysmal atrial tachycardia is the most common.

b) Some severely affected children may have cyanosis due to the admix-
ture of blood through an open foramen ovale.

c) The characteristic murmur is a loud systolic murmur maximal at the
right heart border.

2. Cyanotic lesions associated with increased pulmonary blood flow
a. Total anomalous pulmonary venous return is characterized by partial or
complete anomalous drainage of the pulmonary veins with no direct con-
nection with the left atrium. All blood returns to the right atrium or a tribu-
tary that drains into it.

i. Types of anomalous drainage
a) Infracardiac with obstruction of pulmonary venous return is usu-
ally seen in neonates who present with severe cyanosis and tachycar-
dia and marked pulmonary hypertension.

b) Infracardiac or intracardiac pulmonary venous return with only
mild to moderate obstruction results in intermittent bouts of CHF
that appear to improve with oxygenation and then recur.

Quick 	HIT
Tetralogy of Fallot is the
most common cyanotic con-
genital heart defect.

P E D I A T R I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	355

Pediatric Emergencies
c) Infracardiac or intracardiac anomalous venous return with no ob-
struction results in admixture but only mild or no cyanosis and no
pulmonary hypertension.

ii. A murmur may be present and depends on the degree of pulmonary ob-
struction and the degree of pulmonary hypertension.

b. Truncus arteriosus is characterized by a single outflow vessel arising from
the heart to supply systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations. It is al-
ways associated with ventricular septal defect.

i. In early infancy, only a murmur and minimal cyanosis are present as
pulmonary vascular resistance is high.

ii. In later infancy, when pulmonary resistance falls, signs of CHF predom-
inate but only mild cyanosis is noted. A loud murmur is usually heard
at the left sternal border and is usually associated with only a single sec-
ond heart sound (S 2).

c. Hypoplastic left heart syndrome is characterized by atresia of the aortic or
mitral valve and hypoplasia of the ascending aorta associated with hypopla-
sia or atresia of the left ventricle. All blood flow to the systemic circulation
is via a patent ductus arteriosus. Closure of the patent ductus arteriosus re-
sults in immediate and profound shock and hypoperfusion associated with
pronounced acidosis.

i. A peculiar gray coloration of the lips and skin is seen before frank cya-
nosis develops.

ii. Peripheral pulses are either weak or absent.

iii. A nondescript systolic murmur is often heard but is nondiagnostic.

iv. If a ventricular septal defect is also present, hypotension is not seen but
increasing cyanosis and pulmonary hypertension develop.

C. Differential diagnoses. Few things present so dramatically as an infant with cya-
nosis and lethargy. It is imperative to consider all possible differential diagnostic
points in critically ill infants and not become fixated on one diagnosis to the
exclusion of others. Infants who are profoundly ill should be treated with age-
appropriate antibiotics, pending more definitive cardiac evaluation.

1. Sepsis. Presentation of infants with sepsis or congenital heart disease often
is identical and particularly confusing because of poor perfusion and absent
pulses. Pulse pressure that is widened may be very helpful. Frequently, pro-
foundly ill children with congenital heart disease are placed on antibiotics be-
fore a primary diagnosis is made.

2. Inborn errors of metabolism. Profound acidosis is present in children with in-
born errors of metabolism, and ketonuria and aminoaciduria are often present.

A family history of similarly affected infants can help differentiate an inborn
error of metabolism from a congenital heart defect.

3. CNS disease
a. Intracranial hemorrhage accounts for most presentations that are life-
threatening.

b. Iatrogenic drug administration or overdosage is another possibility but not
in the neonatal period.

4. Hemoglobinopathies. Methemoglobinemia resulting in arterial desaturation
is a rare cause. The arterial oxygen tension (PaO2) is usually normal or elevated.

D. Evaluation
1. Examination. Careful physical examination and prolonged observation of
breathing patterns should be performed.

a. Murmurs. Location and timing should be carefully noted.

b. Upper and lower extremity blood pressure determinations should be
made, as well as a recording of pulses in the upper and lower extremities,
with particular attention to the amplitude and duration of impulse.

c. Skin coloration and capillary refill should be noted.

d. Associated congenital anomalies should be sought.

2. Pulse oximetry should be performed with the patient breathing room air and
100% oxygen.

Quick 	HIT
Lower extremity blood pres-
sures should be higher than
upper extremity pressures;
a decreased lower extrem-
ity blood pressure indicates
coarctation of the aorta or
other lesion resulting in left
outflow tract obstruction.

356 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pediatric Emergencies
3. Chest radiography should be performed, focusing on cardiac size and shape as
well as pulmonary blood flow.

4. Electrocardiography and two-dimensional echocardiography are indicated.

5. Cardiac catheterization may be required for severely ill infants.

E. Therapy depends on the diagnosis.

1. Supportive measures include:
a. Supplemental oxygenation
b. Rapid correction of lactic acidosis
c. Maintenance of normal temperature
d. Arrangement for transfer to a center that is equipped to handle such infants
e. The administration of prostaglandin E1 (0.05 to 0.2 # g/kg/min) for sus-
pected ductal-dependent lesions to maintain patency
f. Dobutamine for patients in CHF who do not have ductal-dependent lesions
g. Medical stabilization prior to definitive cardiac catheterization or palliative
surgery
2. Tetralogy of Fallot. Patients require a special form of treatment when hyper-
cyanotic attacks occur, including the following:
a. Placing the infant on the abdomen in the knee-chest position
b. Administration of supplemental oxygen
c. Administration of morphine sulfate subcutaneously in a dose not to exceed
0.2 mg/kg
d. Rapid correction of metabolic acidosis with administration of sodium
bicarbonate
e. Adrenergic blockade by intravenous administration of propranolol (0.1 to
0.2 mg/kg) for infants with tachycardia and cyanosis
F. 	Disposition
1. Infants with a murmur but no evidence of CHF or cyanosis may be referred
for pediatric cardiology evaluation in a more controlled fashion after initial
telephone consultation.

2. Infants with a murmur and signs of CHF but no evidence of cyanosis must be
stabilized after initial diagnostic evaluation. The patient should be referred to a
tertiary center for cardiology evaluation.

3. Infants with cyanosis on room air who fail to improve following the ad-
ministration of 100% oxygen (as determined by pulse oximetry) most likely
have a cyanotic congenital heart lesion and are at high risk for deterioration.

Transport to the nearest tertiary center should be emergently arranged, with a
physician in attendance during the transport.
```
